comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. (I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says. 

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books on magic these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book on the left is the current edition of my third published book, Inside a Magical Lodge. This was my first really original work of nonfiction; Paths of Wisdom and Circles of Power were both attempts to present the Golden Dawn system of Cabala and ceremonial magic respectively in a clearer and more user-friendly form than existing books had managed, but this one broke new ground; there's still nothing else like it in print. In the years before I wrote it I'd joined the Independent Order of Odd Fellows, an old-fashioned fraternal lodge, and realized that huge amounts of classic magical technique assumed that you had a lodge background -- which next to nobody has these days. So I set out, between the covers of a single book, to explain what lodges are, how they work, and how they can be put to use by aspiring mages.

It's one of my better books, though it badly needed the revision I gave it when it got its new edition a few years ago. Like all the books I consider my best, its sales were decidedly modest. Still, it found an audience, and I still hear tolerably often from people (most of them Freemasons) who find it extremely useful for making sense of the old but still functional toolkit of traditional lodge practice. (If you're interested, you can get a copy here if you live in the US and here if you're anywhere else.)

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed. See you next week!***

At this link is the full list of all of the requests for prayer that have recently appeared across the Ecosophia community. A printable version of the entire prayer list current as of 10/29 may be downloaded here. Please feel free to add any or all of the requests to your own prayers.

If I missed anybody, or if you would like to add a prayer request for yourself or anyone who has given you consent (or for whom a relevant person holds power of consent) to the list, please feel free to leave a comment below.

(Also, if you think you might be interested in having anyone pray in support of your own self-improvement, please have a look at the Ecosophia Prayer List Autumn Special.)
* * *

This week I would like to bring special attention to the following prayer requests.

May the brain surgery that Erika's partner James underwent for his cancer on October 16th have gone successfully; and may he be blessed, healed and protected, and successfully treated for all of his cancer.

May Kyle's friend Amanda, who though in her early thirties is undergoing various difficult treatments for brain cancer, make a full recovery; and may her body and spirit heal with grace.

May Jeff Huggin's friends Dale and Tracy be blessed and healed; may Dale's blood and spinal fluid infection clear up sufficiently to receive a heart valve replacement; may his medical procedures go smoothly and with success; and may Dale and Tracy successfully surmount these difficulties.

Lp9's hometown, East Palestine, Ohio, for the safety and welfare of their people, animals and all living beings in and around East Palestine, and to improve the natural environment there to the benefit of all.
 
* * *

Guidelines for how long prayer requests stay on the list, how to word requests, how to be added to the weekly email list, how to improve the chances of your prayer being answered, and several other common questions and issues, are now to be found at the Ecosophia Prayer List FAQ.

If there are any among you who might wish to join me in a bit of astrological timing, I pray each week for the health of all those with health problems on the list on the astrological hour of the Sun on Sundays, bearing in mind the Sun's rulerships of heart, brain, and vital energies. Back in 2007, I was invited to join a magical working which had as its goal making people on the Right see the Left accurately. This was done by a bunch of Leftists who were convinced (as I was, in those days) that the only reason people on the Right weren't Leftists was that they were brainwashed by the Right. I've since had cause to review my old notes, and came across this and realized that there's probably a good reason why my divination told me not to go there.

I've realized that there were two ways that the target of this working could have been met: the first was to adjust the image the Left had in the mass mind on the Right; the second would be to align the Left with the image held in the mass mind of the Right. The first option, would, however, instantly create a situation of magical conflict, and might not work at all on some of the folks on the Right. Fundamentalist Christians, for example, have some kind of spiritual connection, and were never going to embrace seeing the Left as it stood in 2007 as anything other than satanic monsters.

In the years following this working, the Neopagan scene collapsed morally and spiritually (to match the image the Right held of them), and then a sizable fraction of the Left in general began acting out the lurid fantasies the Right held of it: atheists turns to demonolatry; the pro-choice movement began to embrace infanticide; the tech companies began overt censorship and information control; the "War on Christians" became a lot more real, with things like bans on closing stores on Sunday; and so on and so forth. This was not the intention, but it looks to me as if the working is running its course quite nicely: the image the Right holds of the Left is aligning with the way the Left really is.

All I can say about the scale of the working is that it was done by at a minimum a few dozen groups scattered all over North America; and it does seem to me that the timing is suspicious as well. I doubt that on its own it could have accomplished the complete collapse we see, but it seems odd to me. Is it possible this working plays a role in the radical changes the Left has undergone in the last 15 odd years?

replies.title: Re: Astral oddities
replies.content:
What you describe here was a common occurrence for me, for years, when my astral travel was focused on more shamanic / neo-shamanic approaches, and also during certain Heathen-oriented practices. As you noted yourself, it's the difference between seeing a scene--scrying--versus astral travel itself, and that difference is significant!

One of the impacts of that difference is the distinction between 'receiving,' as you were expecting to do, versus intentionally exploring the vast astral world. When exploring, it's good to have a destination in mind, and it's considered highly advisable to share your journey with one or more spirit-companions, whether these are ancestral spirits, Deities, spirits of animals, trees, or others. You build a daily relationship with this companion, and always enter the astral realm in company with them. They are invaluable as guides, and for getting you out of tight spots, guiding you when you are lost, and helping you interpret what you find and act / react appropriately.

You asked 'how does space work there,' and that's a good question! It can be very disorienting to lack an anchor or a frame of reference, and that's why you want to have your guide with you. Another approach I've used when in the kind of space you describe is to call, with all my being, on the presence of the World-Tree; to deliberately perceive that its boughs or roots are all around me, and I can use them to climb up or down, to return to my point of entry into the astral. If I don't see them, I search for them by feel, or scent, or hear the leaves rustling. The World-Tree reaches across all the planes; have faith that it is there and you will find it!

As for 'walking' where there is no purchase or direction: here it is useful to have some kind of sonic background as you enter the astral: drum, didgeridoo, chanting or intoning, other kinds of music or sounds. Among my favorites are the sound of toads singing on a spring evening, cicadas in the summer, crickets in the fall. You can find recordings of drumming and other kinds of sonic patterns that are especially designed for such travel.

When you begin to feel lost or disoriented, focus on this guide-sound to find your way back to the earthly plane, or to your destination that you decided on beforehand. In this plane you move by thinking / intending to move, not by depending on your physical limbs which are not exactly present there. Having a sonic background that you've trained yourself to alert and respond to helps you stay on track toward your destination or your return.

Diana Paxson, a highly experienced traveler in these realms and long-time Heathen Elder, has a very useful book that covers all the best procedures for this kind of work, called "Trance-Portation: Learning to Navigate the Inner World." I'm glad to read that you're accepting the challenge! You may find the advice in this book makes a great difference as you proceed on the way. It's not without perils and pitfalls, but is a very rewarding experience nevertheless.

Gods speed the journey!
Winifred Hodge Rose

comments.title: More of a followup note.
comments.content:
Back in your notes on Chapter 5 of Levi's Ritual, I asked you about the lights in Carnaki's "electric pentagram".

Well, after reading a paper on William Hodgson's awareness of emerging technologies, I can't say for sure what the bulbs were, but candidates include Geissler tubes, custom Crookes tubes, true Cathode-ray tubes, and Tesla's Plasma Lamp. All of them could run from large batteries with 1900's voltage rectification, albeit not brightly or for long periods of time.

If I had to guess, the true Cathode-ray tube is the sibling of the vacuum tube, a very similar device used for electronic signal switching and radio frequency filtering. This was state of the art filament-free tube technology before "modern" neon lighting.

"Modern" neon lighting became commercially available just after the period Hodgson was writing the Carnacki stories, but the technology trends from Geissler's time on were pointing in that direction.

comments.title: Berserkers

replies.title: Re: Berserkers
replies.content:
1) Good heavens, yes. Many ancient peoples had ways to use shamanistic, magical, or mystical practices in battlefield conditions. Using animal forms was a bit unusual, but there are historical reasons for that -- I discussed them in a blog post here:

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The great secret of magic is that its most effective use is in changing yourself. Yes, you can change your environment to some extent through magical practices, but then it's the same old you in the new environment, with the same old flaws and failings and weaknesses. Use magic to get rid of the flaws and failings and weaknesses, or to give yourself ways to work around them, and the doors open wide.

Let's say you want to become a painter. What's keeping you from becoming one? You need some skills; okay, magic can help you get those. (One of the most successful talisman workings I ever did got me two years of university without a penny of student debt.) You need some opportunities; sure, you can use magic for those, too. But you also need work habits, a clear personal vision, various capacities of mind and eye and hand, and the list goes on -- and all those things can be gained or amplified by magical workings.

Finally, magic is itself an art. It requires daily practice, which starts with simple workings and builds from there. That's most of the magic I do these days -- and I put about an hour and a half a day into various magical practices. Start with that and the buckets will show up sooner than you expect.

comments.title: GD pentagram rituals
comments.content:
Dear Mr Greer,

I'm interested in the "prehistory" of the GD pentagram (and hexagram) rituals in general and in that of the LBRP in particular. There are several questions I'd like to ask. (Please acccept my apologies if my post is too long.)

1. In the SIRP, the GD uses 6 Hebrew Divine names to invoke/banish the 4 Elements + Spirit Active + Spirit Passive. Four of these names (those composed of four letters) are also used in the LBRP. To the best of my knowledge, no definitive rationale behind the LBRP attribution of these four names to the four quarters has been offered.

However, while skimming the Heptameron I chanced on an illustration depicting a magical Circle to be drawn for some of the magical operations contained in that grimoire. I was struck to see that magical Circle featuring three of the four LBRP names in the specific LBRP arrangement – to wit, using a clock as an analogy: AGLA at 9, TETRAGRAMMATON at 12, and ADNY at 3 (the only difference being ELOY, rather than AHYH, at 6).

My question: could it be that the designers of the LBRP borrowed its structure from illustrations of magical circles found in primary sources on medieval/Renaissance magic? And therefore that the rationale behind the choice and specific arrangement of the LBRP names would, in fact, be "as simple as that", rather than the product of (rather speculative) theological/metaphysical considerations sometimes found in GD literature?

2. In the GD, each point of the pentagram is attributed to a particular element. If the attribution of Spirit to the top point needs no commentary, what is the rationale behind the other four attributions? (Earth=bottom left, Fire=bottom right, etc.) Did they originate in the GD, or were they derived from some earlier sources?

3. Agrippa's Scale of four refers to the Archangels Michael, Raphael, Gabriel and Uriel explicitly as the "angels ruling over the corners of the world" and to the four cardinal directions as the "four corners of the world" (both being obvious references to the Book of Revelation), thus providing the following basic attributions: Michael=East, Uriel=South, Raphael=West, Gabriel=North. Additionally, and significantly, the same Scale mentions "the four rulers of the elements" by name and these are explicitly not the Archangels. It would therefore appear that according to Agrippa the attributions of the Archangels to the four quarters is primarily spatial (e.g. Michael=East) rather than elemental (e.g. Michael=Fire) in nature.

My question: what is the origin of Agrippa's attributions of the four Archangels to the "four corners of the world"? Did Agrippa borrow his spatial attributions from a preexisting tradition? Or was this an original contribution of Agrippa?

(Heywood's Hierarchie of the Blessed Angells features the exact same attributions, but that work is much later than Agrippa's, so there is no telling whether Heywood borrowed his attributions from Agrippa or from some independent source.)

NB: I'm well aware that the Scale of four also includes elemental attributions (East=Fire, South=Earth, West=Air, North=Water) and that these attributions follow ancient astrological symbolism based on the elemental nature of the zodiacal signs. But if we were to follow that line of reasoning the question would then become: if Archangel Gabriel is in the North because the North is Water, then why (and by whom / what tradition) was Archangel Gabriel attributed to Water in the first place? (And the same question applies, mutatis mutandis, to the other Archangels.)

Thank you for your time and for any help you can offer with my questions!

replies.title: Re: GD pentagram rituals
replies.content:
The four archangels you name under point 3 are the only four angels named in the Bible: Michael and Gabriel in several places, and Raphael in the Book of Tobit (which is canonical for Catholic and Orthodox Christians, but not apocryphal for Protestant Christians). Uriel is named only in the book called 2 Esdras in the Protestant apocryphas, but (usually) 3 Esdras in Medieval Latin Bibles; it is not found in Eastern Christian Bibles (except for the Slavonic Orthodox Bible). So the pattern of four and only four named archangels is a relatively late development in Western Christianity.

Once it developed, it was easily added to the other "Fours" of Medieval cosmology: the four Evangelists, the four Gospel symbols (Man, Lion, Ox, Eagle), the four points of the compass, the four stations of the sun (solstices and equinoxes), the four seasons, the four humors, the four temperaments, the four elements, the four ages of a human life, etc. etc. So yes, it's a preexisting tradition.

replies.title: Re: GD pentagram rituals
replies.content:
1) That's quite possible. Remember that mages in the wake of Eliphas Lévi's revival of magic were basically piecing together the fragments of a lost and abandoned tradition, they took inspiration wherever they could find it, and very often they simply pieced things together and tried the resulting rituals to see how they worked.

There's a great metaphor in John Crowley's novel Aegypt about that. He wants us to imagine a vast machine of glass and bronze and crystal that was torn apart, smashed on the ground, and the fragments left in the gutter. Then many years later some people started picking up the fragments, trying to reassemble them and figure out how they worked. That's the story of Renaissance magic and the modern occult revival.

2) I haven't seen an earlier version of the GD pentagram correspondences, but that doesn't mean there wasn't one. A lot of occult material was passed from hand to hand by practitioners or within secret societies.

3) Robert's discussion of the archangels covers the material I know about that subject, and I've never looked into the origins of Agrippa's symbolism -- the focus of my research is late 19th and early 20th century magic and occultism. So I'm sorry to say I can't help you here.

comments.title: Calcination
comments.content:
My attempts at spagyric tinctures have been marred by consistent failure with calcination.

After burning the strained vegetable matter to ashes, I put the ashes in a cast-iron pot over a medium flame for several hours. They seem to be reduced to even ashier ashes, and though they appear white when hot, after being soaked in distilled water and then poured through a coffee filter, what I get is pretty dark. Evaporating the water over low heat leaves something brown, and more resinous than crystalline.

The most detailed instructions I’ve found are in the Manfred Junius book. They make me wonder whether the temperature or duration of the treatment in the cast-iron pot has been insufficient. Do I need an industrial thermometer to measure the heat?

I would be glad to learn what I’m doing wrong.

replies.title: Re: Calcination
replies.content:
Fwiw the way I do it is on a container that allows for air flow but not much, like a large pyrex beaker. Then you light it and move it when the alcohol on top is not much, the stirring releases the alcohol underneath.

Then you gently blow it until it turns into hot red coals until no more alcohol remains. You take that into your mortar and pestle and grind it, add more mercury and light it up again. When the salts are red hot again and don't require alcohol just keep blowing the flames and stirring and all the specs have been consumed and reconsumed by the "coaling". I've never achieved white but more like a light gray.

This is Stavish's method from The Path of Alchemy

replies.title: Re: Calcination
replies.content:
If you're getting brown residue, not gray or white, the temperature of your flame is insufficient. What kind of flame are you using? An alcohol lamp emphatically will not cut it, and cast iron isn't the best option -- a porcelain crucible is much better.

My low-cost but quite effective method was to get charcoal going in a backyard barbecue kettle, wait until the flames died down, and then make a well for the porcelain crucible in the middle of the coals and put it there. Charcoal burns at 500° to 700°F, which will give you a very good calcination -- pale gray to white, depending on the herb. (An alcohol flame, by contrast, burns at between 365° and 385°F, which is not even close to hot enough.) A little stirring with an iron rod will guarantee complete combustion.

replies.title: Bibliomancy 101
replies.content:
In my experience, picking up a few verses will cause needless distress, because our mind will make a beeline for the most grim line and latch to it. Instead, my process is something like this:

1. Pray for guidance. Light a consecrated candle and under its light do the following...
2. Pick the book/chapter. Open the Bible roughly in the half, pass my open hand over the left side, then the right side and try to feel what is the correct side. Do not pick whatever page you opened up first, move a chunk forwards or backwards depending on the tingle of your hand. Repeat several times until you feel you pick the track. Do not read as you do this.
3. Pick the verse. When you have narrowed down the text to a couple of pages, move your index finger up and down. Close your eyes, because the temptation to read will be greater and no one can unread anything. The line that feels "hot" is the personal message for you.
4. Open your yes. Read the line immediately above, at and below your finger. Re-read them, but this time picking full verses (either leave a little bit out, or pick a little bit more).

As for interpretation...

5. Read the whole paragraph for context. This is still the Divine speaking, but more in an informational tone and not so much as "this is what you need to do now". Meditate on how this applies to the situation at hand.
6. Read the whole chapter for context, and the foot notes, and the next/previous chapter as needed. Your aim here is to inform your intellect of the whole situation (though you will need to do less of this if you study the Bible regularly and are able to remember the whole passage from the top of your head). Reassess how this all applies to the situation at hand.
7. Go back to the One Verse that is the crux of the message. Pray one more time, read it aloud, discern what is for you to do about the situation.

---------

Now, since I obviously cannot do all that for this post, please take what follows with a grain of salt. But I will point out that the curse is not towards the unfaithful common people who have deviated from the ways of their parents, but towards those who are actively working to suppress the Prophet and the Lord's message. And while the future ahead for the common people looks dark, there's nonetheless the fact that the Lord willed to send a Prophet to warn them, so they would be prepared and not keep making their situation worse that what is already. replies.title: Re: Daylight Savings Delenda Est and Similar Energy Centers Across Systems
replies.content:
If you pay attention to how folks mark the holidays, the mismatch becomes really obvious. Memorial Day cook outs, pool parties, folks thanking veterans for their service, patriotic displays, and so forth. Since it's unofficially the "start" of the summer, everybody's ready to cut loose and enjoy warm weather and community.

Veterans' Day, on the other hand, is all somber ceremonies, dignified remembrance, and pleas to help those affected by war. Sure, folks might do something fun for the long weekend, but it's a lot less likely to be a lively cook out and more likely to be a camping trip or something.

At least, that's been my experience, and as I've learned more about the associations different traditions and cultures have with different times of the year, and the non-physical currents moving through the world, it makes perfect sense. A simple flip-flop would neatly solve the problem, but I don't see that happening without a major outcry.

replies.title: Re: Daylight Savings Delenda Est and Similar Energy Centers Across Systems
replies.content: Answering that question would take a great deal of comparative study that, as far as I know, nobody has done yet. It's like the question of whether the thunder gods of different cultures are the same god under different names, or different gods: a fine quandary!

replies.title: Re: Daylight Savings Delenda Est and Similar Energy Centers Across Systems

replies.title: Re: Daylight Savings Delenda Est and Similar Energy Centers Across Systems
replies.content:
Memorial Day grew out of Decoration Day, which was when Victorians cleaned up cemeteries and laid wreaths and other decorations on graves. (Victorian cemetery culture was a big thing, and there were a lot of dead to be memorialized after the American Civil War.) I don't know if it ever had any connection to Easter, but in Christian culture, Memorial Day in May would actually seem to make quite a bit of sense, because of the link to the ideas of resurrection and rebirth, linked to spring.

Veterans Day used to be Armistice Day (11/11), and grew out of the celebration of the end of the Great War, and veterans' demands for better treatment after that war. Christmas, which takes place around the winter solstice, is a celebration of life and birth (as opposed to re-birth/resurrection), so maybe celebrating the living in the lead-up to Christmas does make some kind of sense in a Christina context as well.

replies.title: Re: Daylight Savings Delenda Est and Similar Energy Centers Across Systems
replies.content: Thanks for this - I knew the history of Armistice Day becoming Veterans' Day, but not where Memorial Day came from.

replies.title: Re: Daylight Savings Delenda Est and Similar Energy Centers Across Systems
replies.content:
FWIW, in Viet Nam, Tet, the lunar new year, and also pretty much the biggest holiday season is in the spring. There are lots of festivities, yellow flowers, lanterns, visiting (so. much. visiting.), some pretty typical new-year type ritual observances (everybody leaves the house before midnight, and the person considered luckiest must be the first to re-enter)

But the other thing you do at Tet is remember the dead. Families clean the graves of their dead, offer incense and prayers at the tombs, there are special observances at the temples and churches.

So... it's very traditional to remember the dead in the spring, in at least one other place. Maybe not so weird?

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I'd like to weigh in to praise this book of our host's. I think so highly of it that I assigned its first edition to students in a few of my small "independent study" courses on magic at Brown University, way back in the day. Lodgework hasn't influenced my own personal style of magic, but a great many of my magical acquaintences owe a lot to it.

One of the things that (IMHO) caused the decline of the old fraternal orders in the USA was the formation of a country-wide social safety net during the many long years of FDR's presidency. Pretty much all of my male ancestors in the 19th and early 20th centuries were active lodge members (Freemasons, Odd Fellows, Woodmen of the World, one of the less occult Druid orders, etc.) in part so that the womenfolk wouild not be left to starve after they died or if they became disabled. (This happened to lots and lots of women back in those days.)

But in addition their obvious value as safety nets for families, the fraternal lodges were also an excellent training ground for young men to become good men, and then better men. There is a very good study of this aspect of lodgework by Mark Carnes, Secret Ritual and Manhood in Victorian America (1991). The loss of this important training gound was clearly one of the harmful consequences of FDR's reforms.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I recently read an excellent article on how lodges and other small, local groups served as training grounds getting things done at all levels of society, which allowed for remarkably robust, decentralized responses to things like the Civil War and the Spanish Flu: https://www.palladiummag.com/2023/03/30/a-school-of-strength-and-character/

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you for this article; it strikes me as excellent! I will be pondering it at length.

In addition to the fraternal orders, here in New England (at least) the other great institution for this sort of training was the Town Meeting, which still survives in many smaller towns.

I was also glad to see that the author highlighted the value of Roberts' Rules of Order, which I first encountered in my 'teens--IIRC, in my lodge of the paramasonic Order of DeMolay (for teenaged boys).

It was a very different world back then, and I am glad that I was fortunate enough to catch the last years of it. At my own University, faculty meetings were conducted according to Roberts' Rules of Order. I was constantly astonished to see how poorly my faculty colleagues understood these rules, or even the logic behind their operation.

For example, someone would propose a motion, and during the ensuing discussion, someone else would propose an amendment to the original motion. Eventually the amendment would be voted on, and then most colleagues saw no need to hold a second vote on the (amended) motion itself, and were constantly puzzled by that procedure. The difference between a vote to change (amend) a motion and the subsequent vote to adopt the motion as amended (or not) seemed to make no sense to them even after the parlimentarian's explanation: "But we already voted! Why do we need to vote again?"

It was about the same time that the populace, who used routinely to be called "citizens," began to be called "consumers" more and more often -- a very noticeable change at the time.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That matches my experience - faculty meetings are run using Roberts's Rules of Order, but it's fairly perfunctory - rare that we get more than "motion to approve minutes," "motion to vote on proposal," and that's about it. The thought that a common fluency in how to get groups of ornery folks to talk things through and come to an agreement can be widely inculcated is a hopeful one, I just hope we haven't come to far, as JMG says about Lodges specifically.

Once, decades ago, I hoped that I could help launch a revitalization of the old fraternal orders; I was very active in the Odd Fellows in those days, and put a lot of work into trying to interest other people in the astonishing possibilities of the lodge system. comments.content:
Hi JMG,

My friend's band is selling t-shirts with the slogan "Remember What They Took From You" printed on the back. From a magical perspective, what do you make of this slogan?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Oof. Brilliant, harsh, and potentially very effective. As a magical intention, it's very well crafted: it's phrased in positive terms and it defines an action that each person influenced can choose to pursue without anyone else being able to hinder them.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I don't know the context, but in a lot of situations I imagine that I'd weaken that by adding an "Also" somewhere (there are a lot of places it could go). Otherwise, it puts a burden on the recipient's own capacity to keep a balanced perspective, particularly in relation to potential psychological displacements of the collective agency of whichever group the recipient might be identifying with.

In the alternative scenario where "They" hadn't taken what they took, would they still have provided something else that the group might not want to admit it had wanted? (Perhaps even something that had itself been taken by "Them" from other groups?) In the alternative scenario where "They" hadn't taken what they took, might that have just resulted in some other group taking what they took instead, or might indeed the group itself have similarly taken something from "Them" instead?

Sometimes it's important to stand up for a higher and symmetric ideal of conduct in a conflict, even when that means restraining oneself, or at least to be clear on when one is or isn't endorsing the principle that the resolution of a given kind of conflict should just come down to main force. A one-sided formulation can make it harder to maintain the clarity required to that.

Maybe it would be more powerful without an "Also", but at the same time, not everyone is up to the task of rightly regulating that power by keeping up their side of that kind of perspectival balance.

Spending alternating periods under the influence of different opposing maxims, such as "Remember What They Took From You" with "Remember What You Let Them Give You" or "Remember What Others Would Have Taken From You In Their Place" or "Remember What They Took From Others For You" or "Remember What You Would Have Taken From Them", would also potentially work for maintaining balance.

There might be some name for that principle of spending alternating periods in different complementary states of mind, but I don't know what that name would be. I first saw it in a book by Agnes Sanford, probably "The Healing Light", which was about practices that she considered to be specialized forms of Christian prayer but that an outsider might term "paramagical". I don't have the book with me, but I think she said something to the effect that time spent in the state of being an active vessel for outward divine help to the world has to be balanced by time in a more passive, receptive, or contemplative state. And then later I saw it in a discussion of successful startup founders and how they time-multiplex manic diligence with depressive realism.

comments.title: Magic, neuroplasticity and the news
comments.content: Hi JMG, and thank you for your work and particularly for making it accessible to all. I read your Primer on Magical Combat, and was especially struck by your insight that only a small proportion of our experience of the world depends on its material realities; 'much more depends on what you perceive, feel, and think about these realities….' I've recently begun to take an interest in neuroplasticity, specifically in relation to tinnitus, and the benefits of letting this negatively-labelled bodily function just be: rewiring the brain by (for instance) not paying attention to that hissing in the ears. Things that we pay more attention to, we get more of, basically. Hence, it seems to me, the benefits of prayer - well prayer that is for good things. A broader view would encompass the media, especially the attention it gives to the worst aspects of our world: the wars, hatreds, celebrity misbehaviour etc. So many of us are 'newsaholics', it seems to me, and our brains are being rewired so that we perceive negativity and thereby reinforce it. Would you see prayer as a form of magic, by your definition? Regards, J

replies.title: Re: Magic, neuroplasticity and the news
replies.content:
Excellent. In their fine satire Illuminatus!, Robert Shea and Robert Anton Wilson had the characters discover that the media and the schools had programmed us all to respond with terror to the word "Fnord," which was all through news stories but never appeared in advertisements; the point of it was to keep everyone in a state of unthinking fear, from which they would flee reflexively by going shopping. It's a wry joke, but (like most of the jokes in that trilogy) it's a funhouse-mirror reflection of reality.

The media exists to keep people full of fear and hate, since that makes it harder to think, and easier for advertisers to trot out images of happiness and security so that people buy useless consumer junk under the delusion that this will make the fear go away. So, yes, throwing away your TV set and telling the mass media to take a short walk off a long pier is a good start. As for prayer, whether or not it counts as magic, it's a very useful and beneficial practice and one I encourage everyone to consider taking up.

replies.title: Re: Magic, neuroplasticity and the news
replies.content: A short walk off a long pier and arrive, where? (I know it was a typo, but the image sprang to mind.)

replies.title: Re: Magic, neuroplasticity and the news
replies.content: Ha! Okay, that's good even for me.

replies.title: On black sheep and dysfunctional families.
replies.content:
Not JMG, but if I may...

Etheric birth is something pretty much every human do on automatic as part of our animal heritage. It is normal that you gain independence from "the vibes" of your family of origin, and failure to do so would be, as a mather of fact, pathological. Think of disabled kids who never learn to function outside of home, and who die shortly after their (elderly) parents. It is failure to launch, cranked up to eleven.

I cannot be sure, of course, but the black sheep child "syndrome" sounds much more like an astral phenomenon. It starts with a emotionally immature parent (therefore, someone who had a traumatic Astral birth of their own), who projects in the child who most resembles him/herself all the aspects the parent feel profoundly guilty and ashamed about. Therefore, in the astral network of (dysfunctional) family dynamics, a black sheep exist to get blamed for all the problems that are ultimately caused by the immature parent. This is sadly common,- and more so these days with all those man-boys and girly-girls out there,- but is by no means the normative experience of growing up.

Failure to outgrow your family (and leave them behind) in those cases could botch your own Astral birth (IMHO), but moving out of your parents home will not bring about emotional maturity by itself. One could argue that, in American culture, moving out at the 18th birthday is a sign that the now adult child is still operating in cultural autopilot, while in other more traditional cultures could be considered a sign of independence and emancipation.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Greetings JMG and the best commentariat on the internet.

To Share:
The question of ancestor work and how it can fit into a reincarnationist framework is something that has come up on more than a few occasions on Magic Monday. I’ve been thinking about this issue quite a lot lately, and after doing a deep dive on various occult books, I think I’ve up with a workable theory on how these two things can indeed work together with no contradiction. Here goes:

What we understand as an Ancestral Spirit is NOT actually the reincarnating individuality of the ancestor in question, but rather an astral remnant from the personality of the incarnation that a practitioner is venerating or calling upon. After the second death is the purgatorial phase of the soul’s journey through the afterlife. Once that happens, the astral body is separated from the individuality (higher bodies of the soul) and cast off into some corner of the astral plane. Much of this castoff astral “shell” dissolves back into its source astral elements, but there is a “kernel” that remains and is “archived” (for lack of a better term) and stored in an astral domain we can conceptualize as a hall of the dead or an astral boneyard; this process could be said to be similar to how most of an interred material body ends up decaying but the bones remain for a very long time.

This archived personality, the “shade”, retains the memories of that incarnation along with some of its habits and personality quirks. Normally, the shade remains totally dormant and inert unless it gets reanimated by way of some spiritual or magical procedure. Ancestor Work is one such procedure. This might involve a practitioner invoking a deity or angel associated with the dead, and that entity (if they consent to this procedure) creating an artificial elemental (a “servitor” of the autonomous type) to pluck the referenced shade from the archive and then wear that shade as a suit of clothing for the duration of the procedure. The practitioner on the Earth plane might experience this “ancestor” as something closely approximate to the personality of the long-deceased personality. Of course in many folk cultures that practice ancestor work, the people believe these spirits to actually be the souls of their ancestors. But according to my theory here, these ancestral spirits lack a divine spark and the real souls of their ancestors have long ago reincarnated in various successive material lives or have graduated beyond the need for material rebirths. Alternatively, ensouled astral spirits, or even deities, likely also have the ability to call up a shade and wear it as a suit of clothing for some period of time, for whatever purpose. (However, when this involves spirits of a vague or unknown nature, this is where things can get dangerous when work of this type is poorly executed or established ancestor work traditions are not being followed).

This sort of practice has worked well in traditional folk societies, since they tend to have very strong family egregores. When there is a strong family egregore, archived shades retain an astral link to the families and culture in question. However, when a culture becomes an urbanized civilization and when eventually in the late stages, the majority of its people become deracinated urban-dwellers, the family egregore tend to become very weak. This is especially true in civilizations like our own where hyper-individualism rides roughshod over community and other collective concerns and forms of organization (not to mention the fact that 1000+ years of hostile Monotheism in the West has destroyed almost all tribal and clan structures, and most forms of ancestral reverence.) However, in many cases, national egregores remain at least somewhat strong and ancestor work involving famous cultural ancestors is still quite workable. Japanese Shinto might be a great example of a preserved ancient tradition that has strongly upheld ancestor work of both types.

I could write a lot more on this but I’ll cut it short here. In a nutshell, I propose that reincarnation and ancestor work need not be mutually exclusive at all. One important disclaimer is that I don’t have any personal experience (Gnosis) on this matter, rather this simply an interesting thought experiment for me. It’s all Doxa and Episteme on my end.

To ask:
(1) Does the above theory sound at all plausible to you based on your own occult knowledge and experiences?

(2) In doing research on this topic, I noticed the Theosophical literature on the astral plane seems very dismissive and condescending when it comes to ancestral practices, effectively writing most of it off as degraded and degenerate types of religion. Theosophists of the first couple generations seemed to have been rather sour on ancient pagan religions as well. Why do you think they harbored these sorts of negative attitudes about traditional folk spirituality? Could their distaste for the sort of Spiritualism that was popular at the time be a part of this?

(3) Could Ancestor Work be classed at a type of Necromancy or does that just refer to when people attempt to call upon the recently-deceased?

(4) For anyone in the comments reading this who has knowledge or experience relating to ancestor work, please feel free to chime in with your insights and correct me where you think I’m either off-base or lacking in information.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Here is another possiblity for reconciling reincarnation and ancestor work:

There is a realm of "existence" wholly other than our familiar realm of existence in the form of matter and energy located in time and space. This other realm has been described as the realm of "a time that is no time, a place that is no place." Some people happen to perceive this other realm on occasion; when they do so, they do not perceive it using any of their embodied sense-organs, nor any skill of their embodied mind, but directly, without any intermediation of thought or sensation.

What they perceive of it, when they do so, is wholly beyond the capacity of any human language to describe, even weakly. It is, strictly speaking, ineffable, that is, it is impossible to put it into any words, or indeed into any categories that come from our familiar realm of existence as matter and energy in time and space.

I like to assume that every reincarnation of each one of us, however long ago we might have lived, or however far into the future we might eventually be born, are all "co-present" in this other realm with every other incarnation and reincarnation: here there is no separation between any of us, neither in time nor in space; nonetheless we are all somehow distinct from one another in that other realm. Nor do things feel static and unchanging there; rather, things seem to be "multiplex" (for want of any word that works better here) apart from all distinctions of time and space.

On this assumption, every ancestor is dead in the realm of time and space, but "exists" (for want of a better word) and is quite accessible to each of us within that other realm, if and when we manage to access it.

And there are people who can access that other realm more or less consciously. Others access it much less consciously, very often by means of ritual, which is the royal road into that other realm.

comments.title: Fancy Geomancy and the Egregor Dance?
comments.content:
A good day to you JMG, and to this magical group!

I've been learning geomancy for the past couple months and have some questions. I have the Celtic Golden Dawn and Earth Magic, Earth Divination, if you believe these address my questions, feel free to say "read more." ;)

1. How do you interpret seemingly opposing figures, i.e. left witness Amissio and right witness Carcer, which seems negative for both querent and quesited, yet it produces the judge Fortuna Major. I've seen similar a couple times, in this case the first house was also Rubeus which some consider an ill omen. I asked a series of questions about various focuses for my efforts, and this reading was for me helping more in the creation of my family's homestead. My untrained interpretation went something like I'm bound up in other passions, and this work would lessen that binding and redirect my energy to yield greater fortune in the end.

2. Similarly, how do you interpret nested neutrals, i.e. two Via witnesses and a Populus judge?

3. Is it functional to read all the houses in each reading or are they better used in direct relation to placing the question in a house and looking for relations? For example when I'm asking about possible paths of employment for myself, I'm looking to the 10th house for relation with the first, but is it useful to look at the 5th house to see about how it would affect my children and the 7th for how it would affect my marriage? I imagine this could be a red herring of sorts as there are so many possible representations for each house, that what I'm reading as about my children could actually be about entertainment or my gardens.

4. Along with the above, I tried doing a daily geomantic reading, "how will today go for me" yet it seems to open ended with little ability to place the question in a house. I attempted to get around this by looking at the whole chart for strong symbols in any placement. It seems like geomancy may not be well suited to this style though. Perhaps it would work better with another condition like "how will work go today" or "how will *situation* turn out?"

5. Would you place starting a business as second house for business deals, or 10th house for employment (even though it is self employment?)

Thank you for your work resuscitating this divination system. I've found it clear and ever more communicative as I add in triplicities, houses, way of points, and relationships.

A final more contemplative question for you and the commentariat...

How do you handle the dance between gnosis and an egregor? As I practice daily and seasonal rituals and study, meditate, divine, and pray; new things come to mind and modifications to practice slip in. Sometimes I'll reference and old book like the Druid Magic Handbook and think I've been using one of the ritual forms and realize that I've added quite a bit and removed a bit, and I wonder at how much I'm losing touch with the shared egregor if I don't "true" myself closer back to the original form. Yet, I also value what has come from my work. I come across this same problem in martial arts where a group of practitioners can become self referencing and ineffectual if they isolate too much, they need occasional infusions of new partners and reminders of the efficacy of the original techniques from old masters.

Would you, JMG, or any one else be willing to share a bit on how you personally handle the dance between personal practice and tradition.

As always, I appreciate you hosting this forum, and your counsel.

replies.title: Re: Fancy Geomancy and the Egregor Dance?
replies.content:
1) Each of those is a fine theme for meditation. You'll learn a lot by figuring out how A + B = C.

2) What's confusing about that? You're the right witness, the situation is the left witness, the judge is the advice given. So both you and the situation are changing, and it's best to wait until things have settled down.

3) The thing that causes most people to misinterpret geomantic readings is the habit of overthinking things, and trying to interpret all the houses is a fine way to do that. You have a question; it corresponds to one of the houses. If there's a mode of perfection linking your house (the 1st) with the house of the question, the answer is yes; if there isn't, the answer is no; the figures in the houses and the figure involved in the mode of perfection, if there is one, give you the details. Stop there and you'll get a clear and meaningful answer.

4) I recommend doing a shield chart, not a house chart, for daily readings.

5) 10th. 2nd house is possessions.

6) I just keep moving to the music...

That can be very useful in the right place. It's just that I've watched scores of people misunderstand perfectly clear readings by overthinking.

comments.title: Astral plain beings
comments.content: Most people here probably know that spirit-beings on the astral plane or higher mostly communicate with what we would call psychic telepathy. So that means an astral plane being can very conceivably read your mind and impersonate a deceased person you knew in physical life through some indirect means of communication. I'm sure an astral-plane being could hear what I'm actively thinking at the moment in my conscious mind. My question is, could an astral plane being, or conceivably even a demon, do a "deep-dig" read of your mind, from which they could dredge up things this person they are impersonating would know about you? Emmanuel Swedenborg once stated that astral plane beings are often tricksters who lie very casually (probably just for their own amusement) to humans contacting them indirectly or through channeling. If that's the case, beings that could possibly read your "deep mind" should probably be taken with a large grain of salt about whom they claim to be!

replies.title: Re: Astral plain beings
replies.content:
I think the answer is clearly a strong "Yes!" if you are asking about the bare possibility.

But when a medium or a psychic seems to know things about you that you've never told anyone (and may even hate to recall in the privacy of your own mind), that's not necessarily such a spirit-being at work. There's a fine art--or maybe it's a gift, or even a curse--to reading a person's body-language and the course of a person's life history, which usually reveal far more about everyone's past than most are comfortable with. The boundaries around one's privacy are very porous. Fortunately, many of us are too polite to look through the pores, and many others aren't aware that it's even possible. One learns very early on to cut the flow on information one can receive back to a manageable level.

replies.title: Re: Astral plain beings

replies.title: Re: Astral plain beings
replies.content: It really depends on the type of entity, and on what they're looking for. Demons understand human cravings, addictions, passions, and corruptions very well indeed, but the higher aspects of our consciousness baffle them totally. Intelligences can figure out our thoughts and memories but seem to find our emotions very puzzling, and so on. There's an old bit of folklore that says that spirits can imitate human beings fairly well, but they always get a detail or two wrong -- keep an eye out for that detail, and of course maintain your protective workings, and you should be fine.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: 30 minutes a day is what I recommend for beginning to early intermediate students -- that's enough time to do a protective ritual, a meditation, and a daily divination. Add a couple of hours of study a week and time for practical workings as needed. As you get to more advanced levels, be ready to ratchet it up to an hour or an hour and a half a day. More than that isn't very useful, and tends to get you unhelpfully detached from practical issues.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG:

Hope you’ve had a nice Halloween season. Thank you as always for hosting this space!

From the POV of traditions like the FHR et al, what is evil? Where does it come from? Does it serve any purpose?

Thank you 🙏🏻

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The problem here is that this word "evil" means quite a range of different things, many of which have nothing to do with each other besides being lumped together in the same category -- and of course what goes into that category varies quite a bit depending on who's doing the labeling! Dion Fortune in her magical writings talks about the difference between what she calls negative evil and positive evil, and we can start there.

Negative evil is the basic cussedness of the cosmos, the resistance we all smack face first into when trying to do anything. It's death, sickness, pain, conflict, inertia. It's necessary for anything to exist at all -- you can't have life without death, health without sickness, up without down, and so on -- and its purpose is to function as something to push against and build on.

Positive evil is imbalance on the part of conscious beings. It happens because we all have at least a little free will, and many of us use it very, very stupidly. Since it inevitably circles back around to bite the perpetrator in the backside, it has a purpose, which is education; by suffering the consequences of our stupidity, in this life or another, we learn not to be such dorks.

comments.title: Self Discipline
comments.content:
Greetings ArchDruid,

I may have asked before if so forgive me, but could you recommend some resources on magic to improve self discipline?

My skillset is still mostly basic levels simple rituals, Hoodoo style herbal working, mantras that sort of thing.

replies.title: Re: Self Discipline
replies.content: This is a question I get all the time, and nearly always what's behind it is a tangle of divided will and mixed motives. Self-discipline won't help you if you're trying to force yourself to behave in ways that deep down, you don't want to do or even know that you shouldn't do. What I recommend instead is a lot of journaling. Every day, write down a few words about one of the things you think you need self-discipline for, and then let yourself write whatever comes to mind, no matter how silly, nasty, shameful, or what have you it might be. Let your subconscious vent. Only when you find out why there's a conflict inside you can you resolve it.
